name: Account Lockout
summary: Application fails to lock accounts after repeated consecutive failed login attempts.
description: The application does not lock user accounts after 5 consecutive invalid login attempts. Affected URL: https://example.com/login.php Impact: A malicious user may gain access to user accounts by submitting a large number of login attempts.
remediation: Lock user accounts after 5 consecutive invalid login attempts.
animated_example: Navigate to the login endpoint and attempt 10 consecutive failed logins with an invalid password
Observe: no lockout is triggered — the application continues to return HTTP 200/401 responses
Use Burp Intruder with a wordlist to confirm unlimited attempts are accepted without rate limiting
Confirm: a valid account is successfully accessed via dictionary attack with no throttling in place
Document: missing account lockout — raise as High severity; recommend lockout after 5 failed attempts

name: Account Lockout - Abuse
summary: Locked accounts have no automatic unlock mechanism, enabling denial-of-service against valid users.
description: Once an account is locked, there is no unlock mechanism. User accounts are locked after 3 failed attempts. Impact: A malicious user may submit a large number of login requests in order to lock out user accounts and deny service to users.
remediation:
Unlock user accounts after a defined period determined by the business.

Implement a step-up CAPTCHA which is presented after 2 failed login attempts. If a CAPTCHA is not acceptable by the business, implement rate-limiting on the login function to prevent one IP address from submitting a large number of login requests.
animated_example: Navigate to the login page and attempt 3 consecutive failed logins for a known valid account
Observe: the account is locked after the threshold but there is no automatic unlock timer presented
Submit the valid credentials after 30 minutes and confirm the account remains permanently locked
Confirm: an unauthenticated user can lock any account by submitting 3 invalid requests, denying legitimate access
Document: account lockout abuse — missing unlock mechanism; raise as Medium severity; recommend time-based auto-unlock

name: Account Lockout - Forgot Password
summary: Forgot password function has no lockout, allowing unlimited automated reset attempts.
description: The application does not lock user accounts from using the forgot password function after 5 consecutive invalid forgot password attempts. Affected URL: https://example.com/forgotPassword.php Impact: A malicious user may gain access to user accounts by submitting a large number of forgot password attempts.
remediation: Lock the forgot password form for a user after 5 consecutive invalid forgot password attempts.
animated_example: Navigate to the forgot password endpoint and submit requests with enumerated email addresses
Use Burp Intruder to send 50 consecutive reset requests for the same account without delay
Observe: no lockout or rate limit is triggered — each request returns HTTP 200
Confirm: the forgot password function accepts unlimited automated submissions with no throttling
Document: missing lockout on forgot password — raise as Medium severity; recommend lockout after 5 attempts

name: Account Takeover - Security Question Bypass
summary: Password reset can be completed without answering the security question, enabling account takeover.
description: An unauthenticated user may reset the password for any account without answering a security question by sending a request to the affected URL with the command parameter set to "reset_pass". Affected URL: https://example.com/passReset.php Impact: An unauthenticated attacker could perform account takeover by submitting a request to the final forgot password step and bypassing the security question step.
remediation: Validate that the user's security question answer and password reset token when setting a new password.
animated_example: Navigate to the forgot password flow and capture the final password reset request in Burp Suite
Submit a direct POST request to the reset endpoint with the command=reset_pass parameter, skipping the security question step
Observe: the server processes the request and resets the password without validating the security question answer
Confirm: any account can be taken over by sending a crafted request to the final reset step without prior authentication
Document: security question bypass — full account takeover possible; raise as Critical severity

name: Anti-Automation
summary: Application lacks controls to prevent automated brute-force or dictionary attacks on forms.
description: It was identified that attacker can perform form based brute force or dictionary attack to compromise the application.
remediation: Implement **rate limiting**, CAPTCHAs, password policies (length/complexity), account lockout with monitored thresholds, and login throttling. Prefer passwordless/MFA where feasible.
animated_example: Navigate to the login or registration form and submit requests using Burp Suite Intruder
Configure Intruder to cycle through a password list with no delay between requests
Observe: the application processes all requests without presenting a CAPTCHA or applying rate limiting
Confirm: no anti-automation control (CAPTCHA, lockout, or throttle) is enforced on the targeted form
Document: missing anti-automation control — raise as High severity; recommend CAPTCHA and rate limiting

name: Arbitrary Cross-Origin Resource Sharing
summary: Server reflects any supplied Origin header, permitting arbitrary cross-origin access to responses.
description: The "Access-Control-Allow-Origin" header is set according to the provided "Origin" header. Affected URLs: https://example.com/getUserData.aspx Impact: An attacker may construct a malicious web site which sends cross-domain requests to the application when visited by the victim and read those responses.
remediation: Do not allow the "Access-Control-Allow-Origin" header to be controlled. Instead, modify the HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing policy to restrict cross-domain access to trusted locations.
animated_example: Intercept a response from a sensitive endpoint and inspect the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header in Burp Suite
Send a request with Origin: https://test.evil.com via Burp Repeater targeting the sensitive endpoint
Observe: the server reflects https://test.evil.com in the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header
Confirm: the application dynamically echoes any supplied Origin value without validating against an allowlist
Document: arbitrary CORS — cross-origin data access risk; raise as High severity; recommend strict origin allowlist

name: Arbitrary Cross-Origin Resource Sharing with Credentials
summary: Server reflects any Origin and sets Allow-Credentials: true, enabling cross-origin credential theft.
description: The "Access-Control-Allow-Origin" header is set according to the provided "Origin" header. Additionally, the "Access-Control-Allow-Credentials" header is set to true. Affected URLs: https://example.com/getUserData.aspx Impact: An attacker may construct a malicious web site which sends cross-domain requests to the application to fetch [Sensitive Data] and read those responses when visited by the victim.  In combination with the "Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: True" header being set, this allows an attacker to extract cookies, authorization headers, or TLS client certificates from the application's domain when a user visits a malicious website.
remediation: Do not allow the "Access-Control-Allow-Origin" header to be controlled. Instead, modify the HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing policy to restrict cross-domain access to trusted locations.
animated_example: Intercept a response from an authenticated endpoint and inspect the CORS headers in Burp Suite
Send a request with Origin: https://test.evil.com via Burp Repeater and include session credentials
Observe: the server returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://test.evil.com and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
Confirm: any origin can perform credentialed cross-origin requests, allowing session cookies and tokens to be read
Document: CORS with credentials — cross-origin credential theft risk; raise as Critical severity

name: ASP.NET debugging enabled
summary: ASP.NET debug mode is active in production, potentially disclosing sensitive server information.
description: ASP.NET debugging is enabled on this application. It is recommended to disable debug mode before deploying a production application. By default, debugging is disabled, and although debugging is frequently enabled to troubleshoot a problem, it is also frequently not disabled again after the problem is resolved. It may be possible that sensitive information about the web sever is disclosed through the ASP.NET application.
remediation: Disable **verbose headers** and debug pages in production, remove stack traces and comments, and expose only minimal, necessary information.
animated_example: Navigate to the application and trigger an error condition via a malformed request in Burp Suite
Observe: the response includes an ASP.NET debug header or detailed stack trace revealing server internals
Inspect HTTP response headers in Burp for X-AspNet-Version and debug-related output
Confirm: debug mode is enabled in production by reviewing the presence of detailed error output or debug symbols
Document: ASP.NET debug mode enabled — information disclosure risk; raise as Low/Medium; recommend debug=false in production

name: Authentication Bypass
summary: All users are granted admin-level privileges regardless of their authentication role.
description: It was identified that all users have same privilege with admin rights.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Log in as a standard user account and capture authenticated requests in Burp Suite
Navigate directly to admin-only endpoints (e.g., /admin, /manageUsers) without modifying any parameters
Observe: the application grants full access to administrative functions using the standard user session
Confirm: no server-side authentication check distinguishes regular users from administrators
Document: authentication bypass — all users hold admin rights; raise as Critical severity; recommend role-based access enforcement

name: Authorization Bypass
summary: Application fails to enforce server-side authorization, allowing privilege escalation via request tampering.
description: It was identified that all users have same privilege with admin rights.
remediation: Centralize authorization checks, enforce **RBAC/ABAC**, validate server-side, and deny-by-default for sensitive endpoints.
animated_example: Log in as a standard user and capture a request to a restricted function in Burp Suite
Modify the request body or header to include an elevated role parameter (e.g., role=admin)
Observe: the server accepts the client-supplied role value and grants access to administrative functionality
Confirm: no server-side authorization check validates the user's actual role from a trusted source
Document: authorization bypass — privilege escalation via parameter tampering; raise as Critical severity

name: Auto-Complete Feature Enabled
summary: Browser autocomplete is enabled on sensitive fields, risking credential exposure on shared devices.
description: An attacker with local access could obtain the cleartext password from the browser cache, if the username is stored as part of autocomplete functionality.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Navigate to the login page and inspect the username and password input fields using browser DevTools
Observe: the input elements do not include the autocomplete="off" attribute on sensitive fields
Submit valid credentials and revisit the login page to confirm the browser pre-fills the fields automatically
Confirm: credentials are stored in the browser cache and retrievable on shared or public devices
Document: autocomplete enabled on sensitive fields — raise as Low severity; recommend autocomplete=off on auth forms

name: Blind SQL Injection
summary: Application is vulnerable to blind SQL injection, allowing database extraction via boolean or time-based inference.
description: It is possible to gain access to database by injecting SQL commands into data-plane input in order to effect the execution of predefined SQL commands.
remediation: Use **parameterized queries / prepared statements**, enforce server-side input validation, limit DB privileges (least privilege), and implement centralized query builders. Add WAF rules for known injection patterns.
animated_example: Identify input fields or URL parameters that interact with the database (e.g., search, login, filter)
Submit a boolean-based payload: ' AND 1=1-- and ' AND 1=2-- via Burp Suite Repeater to compare response differences
Observe: responses differ based on the truth value of the injected condition, confirming blind SQL injection
Use sqlmap with --technique=B to enumerate database schema and extract data without visible output
Document: blind SQL injection confirmed — data exfiltration risk via inference; raise as Critical severity

name: Blind SSRF
summary: Application makes back-end HTTP requests to attacker-supplied URLs with no response returned to the client.
description: Blind SSRF vulnerabilities arise when an application can be induced to issue a back-end HTTP request to a supplied URL, but the response from the back-end request is not returned in the application's front-end response.
remediation: It is recommended to validate and santize the input.
animated_example: Identify a parameter that accepts a URL or hostname (e.g., webhook, import, callback fields)
Submit a URL pointing to a Burp Collaborator or interactsh listener in the parameter value
Observe: an out-of-band DNS or HTTP interaction is received at the listener, confirming the server made the request
Confirm: the server-side request reaches internal or external destinations without the response being visible to the client
Document: blind SSRF confirmed — internal network pivoting risk; raise as High severity; recommend URL allowlisting

name: BREACH Attack Detected
summary: Application uses weak or predictable nonce values in HTML attributes, enabling BREACH-style attacks.
description: Weak nonce value that is used in the html attributes
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Navigate to the application and inspect the HTML source for nonce attributes on script or style tags
Capture multiple responses in Burp Suite and compare the nonce values across requests
Observe: nonce values are static, sequential, or predictably generated rather than cryptographically random per-request
Confirm: the predictable nonce undermines the effectiveness of the Content-Security-Policy and enables BREACH-style attacks
Document: weak nonce detected — CSP bypass risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend cryptographically random per-request nonces

name: Broken access control
summary: Access control checks are missing or improperly enforced, permitting unauthorized access to data or functions.
description: Broken access control is a type of vulnerability that allows unauthorized users to gain access to sensitive data or systems. This can happen when controls such as authentication and authorization are not properly implemented, or when there are weaknesses in the way these controls are enforced.
remediation: Centralize authorization checks, enforce **RBAC/ABAC**, validate server-side, and deny-by-default for sensitive endpoints.
animated_example: Log in as a low-privileged user and enumerate application endpoints using Burp Suite's content discovery
Attempt to access restricted endpoints (e.g., /admin/users, /api/reports) using the low-privilege session token
Observe: the server returns data or functionality that should be restricted to higher-privilege roles
Confirm: no server-side authorization check is enforced — access is granted based on authentication alone
Document: broken access control — unauthorized data or function access; raise as High/Critical severity

name: Broken Functionality
summary: Portions of the application were non-functional during assessment, preventing complete security testing.
description: Part of the application was not functional during the testing period.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Navigate to the affected area of the application and attempt to exercise the documented functionality
Observe: the feature returns an error, fails to load, or produces no response during testing
Document the specific URL, action, and error message observed for the non-functional component
Confirm: the broken functionality could not be assessed for security controls during the testing window
Document: broken functionality — testing coverage gap; raise as Informational; recommend retesting after fix

name: Broken Object Level Authorization
summary: API endpoints lack object-level ownership checks, allowing access to other users' resources by ID manipulation.
description: Unauthorized access to other users' objects can result in data disclosure to unauthorized parties, data loss, or data manipulation. Under certain circumstances, unauthorized access to objects can also lead to full account takeover.
remediation: Enforce **object-level authorization checks** on every request, use indirect references, and validate ownership on server side.
animated_example: Log in as User A and capture a request referencing a resource ID (e.g., GET /api/invoices/1001) in Burp Suite
Modify the resource ID to an adjacent value (e.g., 1002) belonging to a different user via Burp Repeater
Observe: the response returns User B's resource data without any ownership or authorisation check
Iterate a range of IDs using Burp Intruder to confirm the scope of exposed records across all users
Document: BOLA confirmed — horizontal privilege escalation; raise as High severity; recommend server-side ownership validation

name: Browser Cache Weakness
summary: Sensitive application responses are cached by the browser, exposing data on shared machines.
description: Unless directed otherwise, browsers may store a local cached copy of content received from web servers. If sensitive information in application responses is stored in the local cache, then this may be retrieved by other users who have access to the same computer at a future time
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Navigate to a page containing sensitive data (e.g., account details, PII) and capture the response in Burp Suite
Inspect the response headers for Cache-Control and Pragma directives
Observe: Cache-Control: no-store and Pragma: no-cache headers are absent from the response
Confirm: the browser stores a local copy of the sensitive page by checking the browser cache after navigation
Document: browser cache weakness — sensitive data cached locally; raise as Medium severity; recommend no-store headers

name: Brute force Attack
summary: Application permits unlimited login attempts, making accounts vulnerable to automated brute-force attacks.
description: Brute force attacks are simple and reliable. Attackers let a computer do the work — trying different combinations of usernames and passwords
remediation: Implement **rate limiting**, CAPTCHAs, password policies (length/complexity), account lockout with monitored thresholds, and login throttling. Prefer passwordless/MFA where feasible.
animated_example: Navigate to the login endpoint and submit a login request, capturing it in Burp Suite Intruder
Configure Intruder with a common password wordlist targeting a known valid username
Observe: the application processes all requests without triggering a lockout, CAPTCHA, or rate limit response
Confirm: a valid password from the wordlist produces an HTTP 200/302 response indicating successful authentication
Document: brute force possible — no rate limiting or lockout; raise as High severity; recommend lockout and throttling

name: Business Logic - Immutable Data
summary: Application allows modification of fields that should be immutable, enabling unauthorized data changes.
description: The application does not allow the user to edit the login ID. The user may perform this action by submitting a request to the affected URL and modifying parameters. Affected URL: https://example.com/admin Impact: A malicious user may modify system data in a way that disrupts the business.
remediation: Perform server-side validation to ensure that immutable data has not been changed.
animated_example: Log in as a standard user and capture a profile update or account request in Burp Suite
Add or modify the immutable field (e.g., loginId, userId) in the request body via Burp Repeater
Observe: the server accepts the modified parameter and updates the field that should not be editable
Confirm: no server-side validation prevents modification of the immutable data field
Document: immutable data bypass — unauthorized field modification; raise as Medium/High severity

name: Business Logic Error
summary: Flawed application logic allows users to perform actions that violate intended business rules.
description: A malicious user may modify system data in a way that disrupts the business.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Map out the intended business workflow and identify steps where server-side validation may be skipped
Capture requests at each step of the workflow in Burp Suite and attempt to skip or replay steps out of order
Observe: the application processes an out-of-sequence or invalid request and advances the workflow inappropriately
Confirm: the business logic flaw allows an action not permitted by the intended application flow
Document: business logic error — workflow bypass; raise severity based on data/financial impact

name: Cacheable HTTPS Response
summary: HTTPS responses containing sensitive data lack cache-control headers, allowing local caching.
description: The application does not direct browsers not to cache responses which contain sensitive data. Impact: Sensitive data may be cached in local files and retrieved later by a future user of the computer.
remediation:
Set the following HTTP response headers:
Cache-control: no store
Pragma: no-cache
animated_example: Navigate to an authenticated page containing sensitive data and capture the response in Burp Suite
Inspect the response headers for Cache-Control: no-store and Pragma: no-cache directives
Observe: the response is served over HTTPS but lacks caching prevention headers
Confirm: the browser stores the sensitive response locally by verifying it appears in the browser cache
Document: cacheable HTTPS response — sensitive data exposure risk on shared devices; raise as Medium severity

name: Captcha Bypass
summary: CAPTCHA is validated client-side only and can be bypassed by sending requests directly to the backend.
description: Captch is not verified at backend .
remediation: Verify the captch shared by the user.
animated_example: Navigate to a CAPTCHA-protected form (e.g., login or registration) and capture the request in Burp Suite
Submit the form request via Burp Repeater without including a valid CAPTCHA token in the request body
Observe: the server processes the request successfully and returns a valid response despite the missing CAPTCHA
Confirm: CAPTCHA validation is absent or performed only client-side — the backend does not enforce it
Document: CAPTCHA bypass — automated submission possible; raise as Medium severity; recommend server-side CAPTCHA validation

name: CCS Injection(SSL/TLS MITM Vulnerability)
summary: OpenSSL ChangeCipherSpec vulnerability allows a network attacker to intercept and decrypt TLS traffic.
description: OpenSSL's ChangeCipherSpec processing has a serious vulnerability. This vulnerability allows malicious intermediate nodes to intercept encrypted data and decrypt them while forcing SSL clients to use weak keys which are exposed to the malicious nodes. Because both of servers and clients are affected by this vulnerabitlity, every OpenSSL user should update their software immediately. This vulnerabitlity has enough reproducibility and it is very likely for attackers to utilize this vulnerabitlity for target attacks.
remediation: Enforce **HTTPS** everywhere with HSTS (max-age≥31536000; includeSubDomains; preload), disable weak protocols/ciphers, and use modern TLS configuration with valid certificates.
animated_example: Use testssl.sh or sslyze against the target host to enumerate the OpenSSL version and TLS configuration
Confirm the server is running a vulnerable OpenSSL version affected by CVE-2014-0224 (CCS Injection)
Observe: the server accepts an early ChangeCipherSpec message, indicating susceptibility to key material exposure
Confirm: a network-adjacent position would allow interception of session keys using the CCS injection technique
Document: CCS Injection (CVE-2014-0224) confirmed — MITM decryption risk; raise as Critical; recommend OpenSSL upgrade

name: Change password feature missing
summary: Application provides no password change mechanism, preventing users from updating compromised credentials.
description: In the application there is no change password feature and any user can change password for any users without admin rights.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Log in as a standard user and navigate to account settings or profile management areas
Search for a password change or update credentials option within the application
Observe: no password change functionality is available to authenticated users
Confirm: users cannot update their own password, preventing credential rotation after a suspected compromise
Document: missing password change feature — raise as Medium severity; recommend implementing secure password change flow

name: Clickjacking
summary: Application can be embedded in an iframe, enabling clickjacking attacks on user interactions.
description: The application is vulnerable to clickjacking attacks. Impact: An attacker may create a malicious website with an invisible iFrame overlaid on the web application. When the attacker tries to enter data into the web application, the data is instead entered into the invisible iFrame and captured by the malicious website. This may allow an attacker to steal user credentials or other sensitive information entered into the application.
remediation: Set the "X-Frame-Options" header with a value of "DENY" if there is no business justification for allowing the site to be iFramed. If the site must be iFramed from the same domain, set the "X-Frame-Options" header with the value of "SAMEORIGIN". If the site must be iFramed from another domain, set the "Content-Security-Policy" header and set the "frame-ancestors" directive to a white list of trusted sites which are allowed to load the application within an iFrame.
animated_example: Capture a response from the target application in Burp Suite and inspect the response headers
Observe: the X-Frame-Options header is absent and the Content-Security-Policy does not include a frame-ancestors directive
Create a test HTML page embedding the target application in an iframe to confirm it loads without restriction
Confirm: the application renders successfully inside an iframe from an arbitrary third-party origin
Document: clickjacking vulnerability — UI redress attack risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend X-Frame-Options: DENY

name: Clickjacking - Internal
summary: Internally-exposed application lacks framing protections, allowing clickjacking by internal threat actors.
description: The application is vulnerable to clickjacking attacks. Note: This finding is low risk due to the application being only internally exposed. Impact: An attacker may create a malicious website with an invisible iFrame overlaid on the web application. When the attacker tries to enter data into the web application, the data is instead entered into the invisible iFrame and captured by the malicious website. This may allow an attacker to steal user credentials or other sensitive information entered into the application.
remediation: Set the "X-Frame-Options" header with a value of "DENY" if there is no business justification for allowing the site to be iFramed. If the site must be iFramed from the same domain, set the "X-Frame-Options" header with the value of "SAMEORIGIN". If the site must be iFramed from another domain, set the "Content-Security-Policy" header and set the "frame-ancestors" directive to a white list of trusted sites which are allowed to load the application within an iFrame.
animated_example: Capture a response from the internal application in Burp Suite and inspect the response headers
Observe: X-Frame-Options and Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directives are absent
Create a test HTML page on the internal network embedding the target application in an iframe to confirm framing
Confirm: the application renders inside an iframe from another internal origin without restriction
Document: clickjacking on internal app — Low risk due to internal exposure; recommend X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN

name: Complex Password
summary: Application accepts weak dictionary-based passwords, failing to enforce adequate complexity requirements.
description: It was identified that the application not followed the password policy and it is accepted guessable words (dictionary words).
remediation: Implement **rate limiting**, CAPTCHAs, password policies (length/complexity), account lockout with monitored thresholds, and login throttling. Prefer passwordless/MFA where feasible.
animated_example: Navigate to the registration or password change form and attempt to set a simple dictionary word as a password
Submit passwords such as 'password', 'welcome1', or '123456' via the form
Observe: the application accepts the weak password without enforcing complexity or length requirements
Confirm: no client-side or server-side validation rejects common dictionary words as passwords
Document: insufficient password complexity — raise as Medium severity; recommend minimum 12 chars with mixed character types

name: Concurrent login Enabled
summary: Application permits multiple simultaneous sessions for the same account without invalidating prior sessions.
description: It was identified the Same Session is using by number of user at a same time can defined as Multi Login .
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set **HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite** cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.
animated_example: Log in to the application from two separate browsers or sessions simultaneously using the same credentials
Observe: both sessions remain active and functional — the application does not terminate the earlier session
Perform actions in both sessions concurrently and confirm both receive valid authenticated responses
Confirm: no concurrent login restriction or previous-session invalidation is enforced by the application
Document: concurrent login permitted — session theft risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend single active session policy

name: Content spoofing
summary: User-supplied text is reflected in the page without encoding, enabling content injection attacks.
description: Content spoofing, also referred to as content injection, "arbitrary text injection" or virtual defacement, is an attack targeting a user made possible by an injection vulnerability in a web application. When an application does not properly handle user-supplied data, an attacker can supply content to a web application, typically via a parameter value, that is reflected back to the user. This presents the user with a modified page under the context of the trusted domain.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Identify a URL parameter or form field whose value is reflected in the page response
Submit a payload containing plain text or simple HTML: e.g., ?msg=Your+account+has+been+suspended via Burp Repeater
Observe: the injected text renders on the page within the trusted domain context without encoding
Confirm: the application reflects user-supplied content without sanitisation, enabling fake warnings or instructions
Document: content spoofing confirmed — social engineering risk; raise as Low/Medium severity; recommend output encoding

name: CRLF Injection
summary: Application reflects CRLF characters into HTTP response headers, enabling header injection and response splitting.
description: CRLF injection vulnerability exists if an attacker can inject the CRLF characters into a web application, for example using a user input form or an HTTP request.
remediation: Sanitize and **reject CR and LF** in untrusted input used in headers; use frameworks that auto-encode header values.
animated_example: Identify a parameter whose value is reflected in a redirect or HTTP response header
Submit a payload containing URL-encoded CRLF: %0d%0aSet-Cookie:%20session=hijacked via Burp Repeater
Observe: the injected CRLF splits the response and inserts a Set-Cookie header into the HTTP response
Confirm: the application writes user-supplied input into response headers without stripping CR/LF characters
Document: CRLF injection confirmed — header injection and response splitting risk; raise as High severity

name: Cross Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
summary: Application lacks CSRF tokens, allowing forged cross-site requests to perform actions on behalf of users.
description: Cross-site request forgery, also known as a one-click attack or session riding and abbreviated as CSRF (sometimes pronounced sea-surf) or XSRF, is a type of malicious exploit of a website whereby unauthorized commands are transmitted from a user that the website trusts..
remediation: Use **synchronizer/ double-submit CSRF tokens** tied to user sessions, sameSite=strict cookies, and require re-auth for sensitive actions.
animated_example: Identify a sensitive state-changing request (e.g., password change, profile update) and capture it in Burp Suite
Inspect the request for a CSRF token or SameSite cookie attribute
Observe: no CSRF token is present in the request and the session cookie lacks a SameSite=Strict attribute
Generate a Burp CSRF PoC HTML page and confirm the request succeeds when submitted from an external origin
Document: CSRF confirmed — unauthorized state-changing action possible; raise as High severity; recommend synchronizer tokens

name: Cross site scripting (DOM Based-Website defacing)
summary: DOM-based XSS allows script injection via client-side data processing, enabling page defacement.
description: DOM-Based XSS vulnerabilities occur when an application contains client-side scripts that process data from an untrusted source in an unsafe way, without proper validation or escaping. This allows attackers to inject malicious scripts through the DOM, which are executed by the victim's browser. Such scripts can manipulate the DOM to alter the displayed content of the website, commonly referred to as website defacing. This attack can happen entirely on the client side, without any data being sent to the server.
remediation:
•Input should be validated as strictly as possible on arrival, given the kind of content that it is expected to contain. For example, personal names should consist of alphabetical and a small range of typographical characters, and be relatively short; a year of birth should consist of exactly four numerals; email addresses should match a well-defined regular expression. Input which fails the validation should be rejected, not sanitized.
•User input should be HTML-encoded at any point where it is copied into application responses. All HTML metacharacters, including < > " ' and =, should be replaced with the corresponding HTML entities (< > etc).
animated_example: Review the page JavaScript source to identify sinks that read from window.location or document.URL without sanitisation
Submit a crafted URL containing a DOM XSS payload: # in the browser
Observe: the browser executes the injected script from the DOM sink without the payload being sent to the server
Confirm: the vulnerability is DOM-based — the payload executes purely client-side via JavaScript processing
Document: DOM-based XSS confirmed — page defacement and session hijacking risk; raise as High severity

name: Cross-Origin Resource (CORS) Sharing Enabled
summary: Application returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * permitting any domain to read cross-origin responses.
description:
Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) is a mechanism that allows restricted resources (e.g. fonts) on a web page to be requested from another domain outside the domain from which the resource originated. The Access-Control-Allow-Origin header indicates whether a resource can be shared based by returning the value of the Origin request header, "*", or "null" in the response.
If a website responds with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * the requested resource allows sharing with every origin. Therefore, any website can make XHR (XMLHTTPRequest) requests to your site and access the responses. It's not recommended to use the Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * header.
remediation: Is recommended not to use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *. Instead the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header should contain the list of origins that can make COR requests.
animated_example: Capture a response from an API endpoint containing sensitive data and inspect the CORS headers in Burp Suite
Send a cross-origin request with Origin: https://test.external.com via Burp Repeater
Observe: the response includes Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * permitting any origin to read the response
Confirm: the wildcard CORS policy allows any website to make cross-origin requests and read the JSON response
Document: CORS wildcard enabled — cross-origin data leakage risk; raise as Medium/High; recommend specific origin allowlist

name: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) Misconfiguration
summary: CORS policy reflects arbitrary origins, allowing unauthorized cross-domain access to sensitive API responses.
description: Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) is a mechanism that allows restricted resources (e.g. fonts) on a web page to be requested from another domain outside the domain from which the resource originated. The Access-Control-Allow-Origin header indicates whether a resource can be shared based by returning the value of the Origin request header, "*", or "null" in the response. If a website responds with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * the requested resource allows sharing with every origin. Therefore, any website can make XHR (XMLHTTPRequest) requests to your site and access the responses. It's not recommended to use the Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * header.
remediation: Set **Access-Control-Allow-Origin** to an allowlist of trusted origins, avoid wildcard with credentials, and validate preflight responses. Disable reflection of the Origin header.
animated_example: Intercept a response containing sensitive data and inspect the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header in Burp Suite
Send a request with Origin: https://test.evil.com header via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the server reflects the supplied origin in the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header
Note whether Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true is also returned alongside the reflected origin
Document: CORS misconfiguration confirmed — cross-origin credential leakage risk; raise as High

name: Cross-Site Request Forgery
summary: Sensitive functionality lacks CSRF protection, enabling forged requests from malicious third-party pages.
description: The application is vulnerable to cross-site request forgery. NOTE: The Identified parameters and URLs may not list every vulnerable instance in the application. A thorough review of the application's code should be performed to identify other areas that may be vulnerable and should be remediated using the steps in the provided recommendation. Affected Functionality (URL): Edit Profile (https://example.com/editProfile.php) Impact: An attacker may create a malicious website or link to the site which when visited by the victim will result in [sensitive functionality being performed].
remediation:
If your application framework supports it, enable built-in CSRF protection.

If not, ensure that a complex, random, one-time use token is transmitted within a hidden form field in the HTTP request for every sensitive server-side action. The CSRF token should be associated with the user's session and validated on the server prior to performing the action. If the token presented by the user does not match an expected value or has already been used, do not perform the action and instead return a generic error message.

Please see the following guidance from OWASP on cross-site request forgery prevention:
https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross_Site_Scripting_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html
https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Request_Forgery_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html
animated_example: Navigate to the edit profile or other state-changing function and capture the HTTP request in Burp Suite
Inspect the request for the presence of a CSRF token in the headers or request body
Observe: no CSRF token is included and the session cookie does not have SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax set
Use Burp's CSRF PoC generator to create a test page and confirm the forged request succeeds from a different origin
Document: CSRF confirmed on edit profile — raise as High severity; recommend synchronizer CSRF token per session

name: Cross-Site Scripting
summary: User-supplied input is reflected without encoding, enabling arbitrary JavaScript execution in victim browsers.
description: The application is vulnerable to cross-site scripting attacks. NOTE: The Identified parameters and URLs may not list every vulnerable instance in the application. A thorough review of the application's code should be performed to identify other areas that may be vulnerable and should be remediated using the steps in the provided recommendation. Affected Parameter (URL): filter (https://example.com/search.php?filter=<script>alert(1)</script>) Impact: An attacker may create a malicious website or link to the site which when visited by the victim will result in arbitrary JavaScript being run in the victim's browser. This can be used in a variety of attack scenarios, such as to create a phishing attack, perform browser hijacking, deface the site, or disrupt the user experience.
remediation:
Perform input validation on all user input processed by the application. Validate input parameters to ensure the data is of the expected type and does not contain unexpected characters. Return a generic error message if unexpected input is received.

Perform output sanitization on all user-controlled data prior to returning it within application responses. Perform appropriate encoding based on the context the output is placed in. Specifically, use HTML encoding for HTML context, URL encoding for URL context, and JavaScript encoding for JavaScript context.

Do not place user-controlled data directly within a script src, inside of an HTML comment, in an attribute name, a tag name, or in CSS.

Please see the following guidance from OWASP on cross-site scripting prevention:
https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross_Site_Scripting_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html
animated_example: Identify input fields or URL parameters that are reflected back in the page response (e.g., search, filter)
Submit test payload: <script>alert(document.domain)</script> via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the script executes in the browser — no output encoding or CSP is applied to the reflected value
Confirm: the payload persists across page loads (stored XSS) or reflects in the same response (reflected XSS)
Document: XSS confirmed — session hijacking and content injection risk; raise as High/Critical

name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
summary: Stored or reflected XSS allows injection of malicious scripts executed in users' browsers.
description: Cross-site scripting vulnerabilities arise when user input is stored and later embedded into the application's responses in an unsafe way. An attacker can use the vulnerability to inject malicious JavaScript code into the application, which will execute within the browser of any user who views the relevant application content.
remediation: Enforce **contextual output encoding**, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and use frameworks that auto-escape. Avoid using dangerous sinks or sanitize with vetted libraries (e.g., DOMPurify for client-side).
animated_example: Identify input fields or URL parameters that are reflected back in the page response
Submit test payload: <script>alert(document.domain)</script> via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the script executes in the browser — no output encoding or CSP is applied
Confirm: the payload persists across page loads (stored XSS) or reflects in the same response
Document: XSS confirmed — session hijacking and content injection risk; raise as High/Critical

name: CSP Header Misconfiguration
summary: Content-Security-Policy uses unsafe directives that permit execution of untrusted inline scripts.
description: It has been found that unsafe directives has been used in the Content Security Policy Header It has been identified that unsafe directives are used in CSP Header which can allow the execution of unsafe scripts
remediation: Define a **strict CSP** (no unsafe-inline/eval), use nonces/hashes for scripts, and limit sources to trusted origins.
animated_example: Capture a response from the application and inspect the Content-Security-Policy header in Burp Suite
Observe: the CSP includes unsafe-inline or unsafe-eval directives in the script-src or default-src policy
Confirm: the unsafe directives allow execution of inline scripts and eval(), negating XSS protections
Attempt to inject an inline script payload and confirm it executes despite the CSP being present
Document: CSP misconfiguration — unsafe directives permit XSS; raise as Medium severity; recommend nonce-based strict CSP

name: CSP-unsafe directives used
summary: Content-Security-Policy contains unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval, undermining script execution restrictions.
description: It was identified unsafe inline and unsafe eval directive used
remediation: Define a **strict CSP** (no unsafe-inline/eval), use nonces/hashes for scripts, and limit sources to trusted origins.
animated_example: Capture application responses in Burp Suite and locate the Content-Security-Policy response header
Inspect the script-src directive for the presence of 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' values
Observe: both directives are present, permitting inline script blocks and dynamic code evaluation
Confirm: injecting an inline <script> payload executes successfully, demonstrating the CSP provides no protection
Document: CSP contains unsafe directives — raise as Medium severity; recommend removing unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval

name: CSV Injection
summary: Application exports unsanitised user input to CSV, enabling formula injection when opened in spreadsheet software.
description: It occurs when the data in the file is not properly validated prior to export. The attacker usually injects a malicious payload or formula into the input field. Once the data is exported and the file is opened, the spreadsheet application executes the malicious payload on the assumption of a standard macro. This leads to the execution of a payload (command) on target machine which leads to a complete control of the
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Identify an input field whose value is included in an exported CSV or spreadsheet (e.g., name, address fields)
Submit a formula payload in the field: =CMD|' /C calc'!A0 or =HYPERLINK("http://evil.com","Click")
Trigger the CSV export function and download the resulting file
Observe: the formula is present unescaped in the exported CSV file
Document: CSV injection confirmed — arbitrary formula execution when opened in Excel; raise as Medium severity

name: Database connection string disclosed
summary: Application responses expose database connection string details including credentials or server addresses.
description: A database connection string specifies information about a data source and the means of connecting to it. In web applications, connection strings are generally used by the application tier to connect to the back database used for storing application data.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Trigger an error condition by submitting malformed input to a database-backed endpoint via Burp Suite
Observe: the error response contains a database connection string including server name, database name, or credentials
Search application responses and JavaScript source files for connection string patterns
Confirm: the disclosed connection string provides sufficient detail to attempt direct database access
Document: database connection string disclosed — credential exposure; raise as Critical severity; remove from all responses

name: DEBUG Enabled
summary: Django application is running with DEBUG=True in production, exposing detailed error pages and configuration.
description: Django web application is running with debug mode turned on (DEBUG = True ). One of the main features of debug mode is the display of detailed error pages. If your app raises an exception when DEBUG is True, Django will display a detailed traceback, including a lot of metadata about your environment, such as all the currently defined Django settings (from settings.py).
remediation: Disable **verbose headers** and debug pages in production, remove stack traces and comments, and expose only minimal, necessary information.
animated_example: Navigate to the application and trigger an unhandled exception by submitting an unexpected input or invalid URL
Observe: Django returns a detailed error page showing the full traceback, local variables, and settings.py values
Inspect the debug page for disclosure of SECRET_KEY, database credentials, or installed app paths
Confirm: the DEBUG=True setting is active in the production environment
Document: Django DEBUG enabled — configuration disclosure; raise as High severity; recommend DEBUG=False in production

name: Default Credentials Used
summary: Application accepts vendor default credentials that were never changed after deployment.
description: A default password is a password (usually "123," "admin," "root," "password," "<blank>," "secret," or "access") assigned to a program or hardware device by the developer or manufacturer. Although default passwords may help protect from some users, anyone who knows what they are doing can usually guess a default password.
remediation: Eliminate default credentials, enforce strong unique secrets, rotate regularly, and add MFA.
animated_example: Navigate to the admin login panel (e.g., /admin, /login) and capture the login request in Burp Suite
Submit common default credential pairs: admin/admin, admin/password, admin/123456, root/root
Observe: one of the default credential pairs is accepted and results in a successful authenticated session
Confirm: the application was deployed without changing the vendor-supplied or framework default credentials
Document: default credentials in use — immediate account takeover risk; raise as Critical severity

name: Default Server Page Disclosure
summary: Web server default sample pages are publicly accessible, disclosing server type and version information.
description: It was identified that ip with 8080 port is redirected to Web Server Sample document pages
remediation: Avoid open redirects. Use internal IDs mapped to destinations and validate redirect targets against an allowlist.
animated_example: Navigate to the server IP address on port 8080 directly in a browser or via Burp Suite
Observe: the server returns a default installation page (e.g., Apache/IIS/Tomcat welcome page) instead of the application
Inspect the page for disclosed server version, operating system, or installed component information
Confirm: the default server page is publicly accessible and reveals technical stack details
Document: default server page disclosed — information disclosure; raise as Low/Medium severity; recommend removal

name: Directory Indexing
summary: Web server directory listing is enabled, exposing file structure and potentially sensitive files.
description: Depending on the operating system and distribution, the default configuration of Apache allows the indexing of directories. This enables search engines to index your file structure and possibly reveals sensitive data.
remediation: Disable directory listing/indexing on the web server and restrict access to sensitive paths.
animated_example: Navigate to known directory paths (e.g., /uploads/, /files/, /backup/) using Burp Suite or a browser
Observe: the server returns an HTML index page listing directory contents rather than returning a 403 or 404
Review the listed files for sensitive content such as configuration files, backups, or source code
Confirm: directory indexing is enabled on the web server for one or more paths
Document: directory indexing enabled — file enumeration risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend Options -Indexes

name: Directory Listing
summary: Server exposes a browsable directory listing revealing files not linked from the application.
description: .The web server is configured to display the list of files contained in this directory. This is not recommended because the directory may contain files that are not normally exposed through links on the web site.A user can view a list of all files from this directory possibly exposing sensitive information.
remediation: Disable directory listing/indexing on the web server and restrict access to sensitive paths.
animated_example: Navigate to a directory path on the web server (e.g., /images/, /static/) in Burp Suite or browser
Observe: the server returns an auto-generated index page listing all files in the directory
Review listed files for sensitive items such as backup archives, configuration files, or source files
Confirm: directory listing is active and files not linked from the application are accessible
Document: directory listing enabled — sensitive file exposure risk; raise as Medium severity; disable server-side

name: Duplicate headers
summary: HTTP responses contain duplicate headers, potentially causing inconsistent browser or proxy behaviour.
description: Duplicate Headers are there in response headers
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Capture HTTP responses from the application using Burp Suite and inspect all response headers
Observe: one or more headers (e.g., Content-Type, Set-Cookie, X-Frame-Options) appear multiple times in the same response
Confirm: the duplicate headers could cause inconsistent parsing by browsers, proxies, or WAF devices
Review server and middleware configuration to identify the source of the duplicate header insertion
Document: duplicate response headers found — raise as Informational/Low severity; recommend deduplication in server config

name: Easily-Guessable Administration Area
summary: Admin portal is located at a predictable URL path, making it easily discoverable by automated tools.
description: The administration portal is located in an easily-guessable location. Affected URL: https://example.com/admin Impact: Placing the administration portal in an easily-guessable location increases the changes that it will be discovered. Tools such as dirbuster and Burp Suite offer automated content discovery features which simplify this task for attackers. Once the portal has been discovered, an attacker may discover other weaknesses in the system or gain access to the portal through password-guessing attacks.
remediation: Move the administration portal to a more obscure location. Do not use common administration portal names (admin, administration, management, backoffice, trizetto, tzadmin).
animated_example: Run Burp Suite content discovery or ffuf against the target using a common admin path wordlist
Observe: the admin portal is discovered at a predictable path such as /admin, /administrator, or /backoffice
Navigate to the discovered URL and confirm the admin login or dashboard is accessible without prior knowledge
Confirm: the admin portal location is easily discoverable using standard automated content discovery tools
Document: guessable admin path — raises discoverability risk; raise as Low/Medium; recommend obscure non-standard path

name: Easily-Guessable Security Questions
summary: Security questions are trivially guessable or researchable, allowing password reset bypass.
description: The security questions used by the forgot password form are easily-guessable or user-provided. Note: This finding is Medium risk due to the Insecure Password Reset finding. Affected URL: https://example.com/resetPassword.aspx Affected Security Questions: What is your favorite color? Impact: A malicious user may research or guess the security question answer and proceed through the process to reset a victim's password.
remediation:
Ensure that security questions are not easily-guessable or able to be researched on the Internet.

Please see the below guidance on security questions:
http://goodsecurityquestions.com/examples/
animated_example: Navigate to the forgot password flow and review the security questions presented to the user
Observe: questions such as 'What is your favourite colour?' or 'What is your mother's maiden name?' are used
Attempt to answer the security questions using publicly available information (e.g., social media research)
Confirm: the security question can be answered correctly using guesswork or open-source intelligence
Document: easily-guessable security questions — account reset bypass risk; raise as Medium severity

name: Email addresses disclosed
summary: Application responses or source code expose internal or user email addresses to unauthenticated parties.
description: Majority of spam comes from email addresses harvested off the internet. The spam bots also known as email harvesters and email extractors are programs that scour the internet looking for email addresses on any website they come across. Spambot programs look for strings like myname@mydomain.com and then record any addresses found.
remediation: Remove sensitive artifacts, restrict access, and return generic error pages; log server-side securely.
animated_example: Review page source, API responses, error messages, and JavaScript files using Burp Suite's passive scanner
Search response content for email address patterns using Burp's search function or a regex such as [\w.]+@[\w.]+
Observe: internal staff or user email addresses are present in publicly accessible responses or source code
Confirm: the disclosed email addresses are not intended for public display
Document: email addresses disclosed — spam and phishing risk; raise as Low severity; remove from all public responses

name: Encode Used instead of encryption
summary: Passwords are stored using encoding (e.g., Base64) rather than cryptographic hashing or encryption.
description: Sensitive information about passwords are encoded and stored.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Obtain or observe password values returned in responses or stored data (e.g., via SQL injection or direct DB access)
Attempt to decode the values using Base64 or similar encoding schemes
Observe: the decoded output produces plaintext passwords, confirming encoding rather than encryption was used
Confirm: the application stores passwords in a reversible format with no cryptographic hashing applied
Document: encoding used instead of encryption — plaintext credential exposure; raise as Critical severity; recommend bcrypt

name: Error Messages
summary: Application returns verbose error messages that disclose sensitive data or internal implementation details.
description: The application returns verbose error messages which contain SENSITIVE DATA when the user ACTION WHICH TRIGGERS ERROR. Affected URL: https://example.com/userLookup.php Impact: Disclosing SENSITIVE DATA  allows for malicious users to perform more targeted attacks.
remediation: Catch all exceptions by returning a generic error message such as "An unexpected error has occurred".
animated_example: Submit unexpected or malformed input to application endpoints (e.g., invalid data types, boundary values) via Burp
Observe: the error response contains sensitive data such as stack traces, file paths, SQL queries, or user details
Note the specific sensitive data exposed and the URL and parameter that triggered the verbose error
Confirm: the application does not catch exceptions gracefully and returns implementation details to the client
Document: verbose error messages — information disclosure; raise as Low/Medium severity; recommend generic error responses

name: Execution After Redirect (EAR)
summary: Application continues executing and serving content after issuing a redirect, bypassing access controls.
description: It is an attack where an attacker ignores redirects and retrieves sensitive content intended for authenticated users. A successful EAR exploit can lead to complete compromise of the application.
remediation: It is recommended that proper termination should be performed after redirects.
animated_example: Navigate to a restricted endpoint as an unauthenticated user and capture the redirect response in Burp Suite
In Burp Repeater, follow the request that triggers the 302 redirect and examine the response body
Observe: the response body contains sensitive content (e.g., user data, admin HTML) despite the redirect status code
Confirm: the application renders the protected page content before issuing the redirect, allowing interception
Document: EAR confirmed — access control bypass via redirect; raise as High severity; recommend die/exit after redirect

name: Filepath Disclosure
summary: Application reveals internal server file paths through error messages or responses.
description: This SmartAttack reports each page on which it finds path disclosure vulnerabilities, that is, where bad input can lead to an internal application error which reveals the path information. This SmartAttack exploits the application business logic to reveal path disclosures.
remediation: Remove sensitive artifacts, restrict access, and return generic error pages; log server-side securely.
animated_example: Submit malformed or unexpected input to various endpoints to trigger application errors via Burp Suite
Observe: the error response includes absolute server file paths (e.g., /var/www/html/app/ or C:\inetpub\wwwroot\)
Review all error responses and JavaScript files in Burp's site map for path disclosure patterns
Confirm: the disclosed file path reveals server directory structure useful for targeted attacks
Document: filepath disclosure — server structure exposed; raise as Low/Medium severity; recommend generic error handling

name: Forceful Browsing
summary: Restricted pages are accessible by directly navigating to their URLs without proper authorisation checks.
description: The user may forcefully browse to the affected URL in order to [access sensitive data or functionality]. NOTE: The Identified parameters and URLs may not list every vulnerable instance in the application. A thorough review of the application's code should be performed to identify other areas that may be vulnerable and should be remediated using the steps in the provided recommendation. Affected URL: https://example.com/manageUsers.aspx Impact: A malicious user can [access sensitive data or functionality] without proper authorization.
remediation: Perform server-side authorization checks prior to granting access to restricted functionality.
animated_example: Log in as a low-privileged user and note the URLs of restricted pages or admin functions
Directly navigate to restricted URLs (e.g., /manageUsers.aspx, /reports/all) without following the normal workflow
Observe: the server serves the restricted page content without performing a server-side authorisation check
Confirm: the access control relies solely on UI hiding rather than server-side enforcement
Document: forceful browsing — unauthorised access to restricted pages; raise as High severity

name: Host Header Injection
summary: Application trusts and uses the HTTP Host header value in password reset links and other operations.
description: In many cases, developers are trusting the HTTP Host header value and using it to generate links, import scripts and even generate password resets links with its value. This is a very bad idea, because the HTTP Host header can be controlled by an attacker. This can be exploited using web-cache poisoning and by abusing alternative channels like password reset emails. An attacker can manipulate the Host header as seen by the web application and cause the application to behave in unexpected ways.
remediation: Do not trust the Host header. Validate against a server-side allowlist and use server_name. Configure a default vhost that rejects unknown hosts.
animated_example: Capture a password reset request in Burp Suite and modify the Host header to a controlled domain (e.g., evil.com)
Submit the modified request and trigger a password reset email to a test account
Observe: the password reset email contains a link referencing the injected Host header value (evil.com/reset?token=...)
Confirm: the application uses the client-supplied Host header to construct URLs without server-side validation
Document: Host header injection — password reset link hijacking risk; raise as High severity

name: Host Overriding (External Service Interaction)
summary: Application uses a user-supplied host parameter to make outbound requests, enabling SSRF via host override.
description:
The server may be used to send [DNS,HTTP] requests to other external or internal resources. Affected URL: https://example.com/lookup.php?site=http://www.google.com Impact: An attacker may gain access to restricted internal resources by abusing the reverse proxy server's trust of the supplied hostname.
Additionally, an attacker can perform server-side request forgery attacks and leverage the host to perform denial of service attacks.
remediation:
You should review the purpose and intended use of the relevant application functionality, and determine whether the ability to trigger arbitrary external service interactions is intended behavior. If so, you should be aware of the types of attacks that can be performed via this behavior and take appropriate measures. These measures might include blocking network access from the application server to other internal systems, and hardening the application server itself to remove any services available on the local loopback adapter.
If the ability to trigger arbitrary external service interactions is not intended behavior, then you should implement a whitelist of permitted services and hosts, and block any interactions that do not appear on this whitelist.
animated_example: Identify a parameter that accepts a URL or hostname (e.g., site=, host=, url=) and capture the request in Burp Suite
Replace the parameter value with a Burp Collaborator URL and submit the request
Observe: an HTTP or DNS interaction is received at the Collaborator endpoint, confirming the server fetches the URL
Attempt to supply internal addresses (e.g., http://localhost, http://192.168.1.1) to probe internal services
Document: host override / external service interaction — SSRF risk; raise as High severity; recommend URL allowlisting

name: HTML Injection
summary: User input is reflected as raw HTML in responses, allowing injection of arbitrary HTML content.
description: HTML injection attack is discovered in your web application. This is seller to XSS, While in the XSS vulnerability the attacker can inject and execute JavaScript code, the HTML injection attack only allows the injection of certain HTML tags. When an application does not properly handle user supplied data, an attacker can supply valid HTML code, typically via a parameter value, and inject their own content into the page.
remediation: Enforce **contextual output encoding**, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and use frameworks that auto-escape. Avoid using dangerous sinks or sanitize with vetted libraries (e.g., DOMPurify for client-side).
animated_example: Identify a parameter reflected in the page response (e.g., error message, search query, redirect text)

Submit an HTML payload such as

Test Injection

or Click here via Burp Repeater
Observe: the injected HTML renders in the browser as formatted content rather than being displayed as escaped text
Confirm: user-supplied HTML is reflected without encoding, allowing content manipulation in the trusted domain context
Document: HTML injection confirmed — phishing and content spoofing risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend output encoding

name: HTTP Method Misconfiguration
summary: API endpoints accept HTTP methods (e.g., GET for POST-only routes) that should not be permitted.
description: It has been identified that the POST request api endpoint is accessible n the browser whoch is otherwise not accessible.
remediation: It is recommended to validate the HTTP methodds before calling.
animated_example: Identify API endpoints that should accept only specific HTTP methods (e.g., POST-only endpoints)
Send alternative HTTP method requests (e.g., GET, PUT, DELETE) to those endpoints via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the server processes the alternative method request and returns a valid response or executes the action
Confirm: the endpoint does not validate the HTTP method server-side and accepts unintended methods
Document: HTTP method misconfiguration — raise as Medium severity; recommend strict method validation per endpoint

name: HTTP Verb Tampering
summary: Application accepts HTTP verbs interchangeably, allowing state changes via unintended methods.
description: While GET should only be responsible for retrieving data from the server, POST can be used to send data to the server and thus change the server state in this it could be interchangeably. This is not only a violation of standards, but can have several security implications which are discussed below
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Identify an endpoint that should only accept GET and attempt to send the same request using POST, PUT, and DELETE via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the server processes the alternative HTTP verb and performs the corresponding action without method validation
Confirm: state-changing operations are possible via HTTP methods not intended by the application design
Review access controls to confirm they are applied per-method or whether verb tampering bypasses them
Document: HTTP verb tampering — raise as Medium severity; recommend strict server-side HTTP method validation per endpoint

name: Implement Session Time Out Policy
summary: Application sessions do not expire after inactivity, leaving authenticated sessions open indefinitely.
description: Session timeout represents the event occuring when a user do not perform any action on a web site during a interval (defined by web server). The event, on server side, change the status of the user session to 'invalid' (ie. "not used anymore") and instruct the web server to destroy it (deleting all data contained into it).
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set **HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite** cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.
animated_example: Log in to the application and capture the session token, then leave the session idle for 30+ minutes
After the inactivity period, use the captured session token in a new request via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the server accepts the session token and returns authenticated content despite the inactivity period
Confirm: no server-side session timeout is enforced — idle sessions remain valid indefinitely
Document: missing session timeout — raise as Medium severity; recommend 15-30 minute inactivity timeout

name: Improper Error Handling
summary: Application returns detailed error messages including file paths, disclosing internal server information.
description: Potential vulnerability within your web application/server due to the presence of a fully qualified path name to the root of your system. This most often occurs in context of an error being produced by the web application/server. This information will help the attacker to understand about the application to enumerate further.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Submit malformed or unexpected input to application endpoints to trigger error conditions via Burp Suite
Observe: the error response includes fully-qualified server file paths (e.g., /var/www/html/ or C:\inetpub\wwwroot\)
Review Burp's site map and passive scan results for additional path disclosure occurrences
Confirm: the disclosed paths reveal internal directory structure useful for further targeted testing
Document: improper error handling — path disclosure; raise as Low/Medium severity; recommend generic error pages in production

name: Improper Input Validation
summary: Application processes user-supplied input without server-side validation, accepting malicious payloads.
description: It has been identified that the user is able to supply some malicious payload and the server process the request without any validation.
remediation: It is recommended to perform server side validation to sanitize the user input
animated_example: Identify input fields or API parameters and submit unexpected data types, special characters, and boundary values via Burp Suite
Observe: the server processes all submitted values without rejecting invalid or malicious content
Confirm: no server-side input validation or sanitisation is applied to the targeted parameter
Test with injection payloads (XSS, SQLi, command injection) to assess the full impact of missing validation
Document: improper input validation — raise as High severity; recommend strict server-side allowlist validation

name: Improper Inventory Management
summary: Legacy API versions remain accessible and may expose sensitive data or deprecated security controls.
description: It has been identified that older version API inventories still exist and it can pose security threats if it contains any sensitive information
remediation: It is recommended to remove older version APIs that are not used
animated_example: Review the application for version indicators in URLs (e.g., /api/v1/, /api/v2/) and enumerate older API versions
Send requests to legacy API endpoints (e.g., /api/v1/users) that are no longer actively maintained
Observe: the older API version responds and may return sensitive data or lack current security controls
Confirm: the legacy API is accessible to authenticated or unauthenticated users and not retired from production
Document: improper inventory management — stale API version exposed; raise as Medium/High severity; recommend decommissioning

name: Information Disclosure
summary: Application responses contain sensitive data that should not be returned to clients.
description: The application discloses SENSITIVE DATA within responses. Affected URL: https://example.com/userLookup.php Impact: Disclosing sensitive data, such as SENSITIVE DATA, allows for malicious users to perform more targeted attacks.
remediation: Remove the SENSITIVE DATA from application responses.
animated_example: Browse the application while proxying through Burp Suite and review all API and page responses
Observe: responses contain sensitive data such as PII, internal tokens, hashed credentials, or system details
Identify the specific endpoint and response field that discloses the sensitive information
Confirm: the disclosed data is not required for the client-side function and is unnecessarily exposed
Document: information disclosure — sensitive data in responses; raise as High severity; recommend data minimisation

name: Input Autocomplete
summary: Autocomplete is enabled on sensitive input fields, risking credential exposure on shared devices.
description:
The autocomplete feature is not disabled on the FORMNAME form. Affected URL (Form Description): https://example.com/PlaceWhereFormExists.aspx (Login) Impact: A malicious user may obtain sensitive information, such as usernames and PII, from data stored in the browser history.

Publicly-accessible computers and kiosks with multiple users are locations where this data is exposed to others.
remediation: Disable autocomplete for sensitive form fields by including the autocomplete=off attribute to the HTML form or individual input tags.
animated_example: Navigate to forms containing sensitive fields (login, registration, profile) and inspect the HTML source via DevTools
Observe: sensitive input fields (username, password, SSN) lack the autocomplete='off' attribute
Submit credentials and revisit the page to confirm the browser auto-fills the fields from stored history
Confirm: sensitive data is stored in browser cache and retrievable on shared or public devices
Document: input autocomplete enabled — raise as Low severity; recommend autocomplete=off on all sensitive form fields

name: Insecure Data in Local Storage
summary: Application stores sensitive data in browser localStorage, accessible to any JavaScript running on the page.
