description: The application sends the user's [sensitive data] within the the browser's local storage. Impact: Sensitive data stired in the browser's local storage may be leaked to an unauthorized user through the cross-site scripting attacks.
remediation: Do not store sensitive data within the browser's local storage.
animated_example: Log in to the application and open browser DevTools, navigating to Application > Local Storage
Observe: sensitive data such as session tokens, user IDs, PII, or authentication tokens are stored in localStorage
Confirm: any JavaScript executing on the page (including injected via XSS) can read the stored sensitive data
Verify the data persists after closing and reopening the browser tab
Document: sensitive data in localStorage — XSS exfiltration risk; raise as High severity; recommend HttpOnly cookies instead

name: Insecure Data Transmission
summary: Sensitive data is transmitted over unencrypted HTTP rather than HTTPS, enabling interception.
description: Information transmitted over an unencrypted channel. This information should always be transferred via an encrypted channel (HTTPS) to avoid being intercepted by malicious users.
remediation: Enforce **HTTPS** everywhere with HSTS (max-ageâ‰¥31536000; includeSubDomains; preload), disable weak protocols/ciphers, and use modern TLS configuration with valid certificates.
animated_example: Intercept traffic between the client and server using Burp Suite or review the application for HTTP links
Submit sensitive data (credentials, PII) and observe whether the transmission occurs over plain HTTP
Observe: the request is sent in cleartext with no TLS encryption applied to the channel
Confirm: an on-path observer could read or modify credentials and session data in transit
Document: insecure data transmission — raise as High severity; recommend enforcing HTTPS with HSTS

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
summary: Direct object references in API parameters lack ownership checks, enabling horizontal privilege escalation.
description: Attacker, who is an authorized system user, simply changes a parameter value that directly refers to a system object to another object the user isnâ€™t authorized for. Such flaws can compromise all the data that can be referenced by the parameter
remediation: Enforce **object-level authorization checks** on every request, use indirect references, and validate ownership on server side.
animated_example: Log in as a standard user and capture a request referencing a direct object ID (e.g., GET /api/profile/1001) in Burp Suite
Modify the object ID to adjacent values (e.g., 1002, 1003) belonging to other users via Burp Repeater
Observe: the server returns data belonging to other users without validating ownership
Use Burp Intruder to iterate a range of IDs and confirm the breadth of exposed records
Document: IDOR confirmed — horizontal privilege escalation; raise as High severity; recommend server-side ownership checks

name: Insecure Frame (External)
summary: Application embeds third-party content in iframes without sandbox restrictions, risking script execution in parent context.
description: IFrame sandboxing enables a set of additional restrictions for the content within a frame in order to restrict its potentially malicious code from causing harm to the web page that embeds it. The Same Origin Policy (SOP) will prevent JavaScript code from one origin from accessing properties and functions - as well as HTTP responses - of different origins. The access is only allowed if the protocol, port and also the domain match exactly.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Review page source for iframe elements embedding external or third-party URLs
Inspect the iframe tags for the sandbox attribute and permitted sandbox permissions
Observe: external content is loaded in iframes without sandbox restrictions, allowing script execution
Confirm: a compromised or malicious third-party page could execute scripts in the context of the parent page
Document: insecure external iframe — third-party script risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend sandbox attribute on iframes

name: Insecure HTTP methods enabled
summary: Server exposes dangerous HTTP methods (e.g., PUT, DELETE, TRACE) that are not required for application function.
description: HTTP OPTIONS method is enabled on this web server. The OPTIONS method provides a list of the methods that are supported by the web server; it represents a request for information about the communication options available on the request/response chain identified by the Request-URI.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Send an HTTP OPTIONS request to the web server and examine the Allow header in the response via Burp Suite
Observe: the server returns a list of permitted methods including potentially dangerous ones such as PUT, DELETE, or TRACE
Attempt to use the enabled methods on application endpoints to assess exploitability
Confirm: methods that are not required for application functionality are enabled on the server
Document: insecure HTTP methods enabled — raise as Medium severity; recommend disabling all unnecessary HTTP methods

name: Insecure Key Storage
summary: Encryption key used to protect user credentials is stored in plaintext on the web server.
description:
The password used to encrypt user credentials stored within the database is stored in plain text on the web server. Impact: A malicious user who gains access to the key may decrypt user passwords stored within the database.

The key may be disclosed through other vulnerabilities in the application, such as local file inclusion.
remediation:
Protect keys stored in files by encrypting them with a machine certificate.

Alternatively, leverage a secure key vault and programmatically retrieve the key.
animated_example: Review application configuration files, source code, and web-accessible paths for key storage locations
Attempt to access configuration files (e.g., web.config, .env, config.php) via Burp Suite or directory traversal
Observe: an encryption key used to protect stored credentials is present in plaintext in a file or response
Confirm: the disclosed key could be used to decrypt all stored user passwords if database access is obtained
Document: insecure key storage — credential decryption risk; raise as Critical severity; recommend a dedicated key vault

name: Insecure Password Reset - Insufficient Questions
summary: Password reset relies on a single security question, which is insufficient to prevent account takeover.
description: The application only uses a single security question when performing a password reset. Affected URL: https://example.com/resetPassword.aspx Impact: A malicious user may guess the security question answer and proceed through the process to reset a victim's password.
remediation: Ensure that two security questions are both answered correctly simultaneously in order for the user to proceed through the password reset process.
animated_example: Navigate to the password reset flow and observe the number of security questions required to proceed
Observe: only a single security question is presented before the user is permitted to reset their password
Attempt to answer the question using publicly available information about the target account
Confirm: a single guessable or researchable answer grants full password reset access
Document: insufficient security questions — single factor reset; raise as Medium severity; recommend two questions or OTP

name: Insecure Password Reset - No Email
summary: Password reset allows completion via security questions alone with no email verification sent to the account owner.
description: The application allows the user to reset a password by only providing the answers to security questions. No email is sent to the user’s email inbox to verify they are the owner of the inbox. Affected URL: https://example.com/resetPassword.aspx Impact: A malicious user may guess the security question answers and proceed through the process to reset a victim's password.
remediation:
Send the user a forgot password email with a URL which contains a one-time use, cryptographically secure token. Once this URL is visited, present the user two security questions and ensure they are answered correctly.

Ensure security question answers are normalized and then hashed prior to being stored, in accordance with the Cognizant Global Cryptography Standard (https://be.cognizant.com/documents/preview/875834/Global-Cryptography-Standard).
animated_example: Navigate to the forgot password flow and complete the reset process using only the security question answers
Observe: no verification email is sent to the registered account owner during the reset process
Confirm: password reset can be completed without the account owner receiving any notification or verification link
Assess the difficulty of guessing the security question answers using open-source intelligence
Document: password reset without email verification — account takeover risk; raise as High severity; recommend OTP or email token

name: Insecure Password Storage
summary: User passwords are stored using the same AES encryption key rather than individual salted password hashes.
description:
The application does not store user passwords using an appropriate password strengthening hash function, such as bcrypt. Instead, the application stores all user passwords in an AES encrypted format encrypted with the same key. Impact: A malicious user who gains access to encrypted password values may attempt to crack all passwords simultaneously, due to all passwords being encrypted with the same key.

Encrypted password values may be disclosed through other vulnerabilities in the application, such as SQL injection.
remediation:
Protect user passwords by only storing salted and strengthened password hashes. Use an appropriate password strengthening algorithm, such as bcrypt.

Please see the below guidance from OWASP on password storage best practices:
https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet.html
animated_example: If database access is obtained (e.g., via SQL injection), examine the password column storage format
Observe: password values are stored as AES-encrypted ciphertext encrypted with a single shared key
Confirm: all passwords share the same key, meaning a single key disclosure enables decryption of all stored passwords
Cross-reference the finding with key storage practices — confirm the key is accessible on the server
Document: insecure password storage — bulk decryption risk; raise as Critical severity; recommend bcrypt with individual salts

name: Insecure TLS Version Enabled
summary: Server supports deprecated TLS versions vulnerable to known downgrade and decryption attacks.
description: An attacker may be able to exploit this problem to conduct man-in-the-middle attacks and decrypt communications between the affected service and clients.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported **stable version** and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.
animated_example: Use testssl.sh or sslyze to enumerate TLS protocol versions and cipher suites supported by the target server
Observe: the server accepts TLS 1.0 or TLS 1.1 connections in addition to modern TLS 1.2/1.3
Confirm: the deprecated protocol versions are susceptible to known downgrade attacks (e.g., POODLE, BEAST)
Verify whether HSTS is configured to prevent initial HTTP connections that could be downgraded
Document: insecure TLS version enabled — raise as High severity; recommend disabling TLS 1.0/1.1 and enabling TLS 1.2+ only

name: Insufficient Session Expiration
summary: Session tokens remain valid well beyond their useful lifetime, enabling reuse of captured credentials.
description: Insufficient session expiration, a web application vulnerability, allows attackers to reuse old session credentials or IDs, potentially gaining unauthorized access and escalating security risks
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set **HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite** cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.
animated_example: Log in and capture the session token, then wait beyond any stated expiration period before using it again
Submit the captured token in a new request via Burp Suite Repeater after an extended period
Observe: the server accepts the long-lived token and returns authenticated content despite the elapsed time
Confirm: no absolute or idle session expiration is enforced server-side
Document: insufficient session expiration — raise as Medium severity; recommend absolute and idle timeout controls

name: Internal IP Disclosure
summary: Application responses reveal internal IP addresses that can aid infrastructure enumeration.
description: The page source discloses an internal IP address within application responses. Affected URL: Impact: Internal IP addresses may provide enticement information for an attacker which could be used to perform attacks against company resources.
remediation: Remove all internal IP addresses from application responses.
animated_example: Browse the application while proxying through Burp Suite and review all response headers and body content
Search response bodies and headers for internal IP address patterns (e.g., 10.x.x.x, 192.168.x.x, 172.16.x.x)
Observe: one or more responses contain internal IP addresses not intended for external disclosure
Confirm: the disclosed IP addresses are internal network addresses that aid infrastructure reconnaissance
Document: internal IP disclosure — raise as Low/Medium severity; recommend removing internal addresses from all responses

name: Internal IP Disclosure - BigIP Cookie
summary: F5 BigIP load balancer cookie exposes encoded internal IP address of the backend server.
description: The page source discloses an internal IP address within a cookie. Affected Cookie: Impact: Internal IP addresses may provide enticement information for an attacker which could be used to perform attacks against company resources.
remediation:
Configure cookie encryption on the F5 load balancer.

Vendor Reference:
https://support.f5.com/csp/article/K14784
animated_example: Capture HTTP responses and inspect all Set-Cookie headers in Burp Suite
Observe: a BigIP cookie (e.g., BIGipServer...) is present containing an encoded value
Decode the cookie value using a BigIP decoder tool to extract the internal server IP and port
Confirm: the decoded IP is an internal network address not intended for external exposure
Document: BigIP cookie internal IP disclosure — raise as Low severity; recommend enabling cookie encryption on the F5 device

name: Invalid SSL Certificate
summary: SSL certificate is invalid, self-signed, or expired, preventing trust verification by clients.
description: An SSL certificate can be created and signed by anyone. You should have a valid SSL certificate to make your visitors sure about the secure communication between your website and them. If you have an invalid certificate, your visitors will have trouble distinguishing between your certificate and those of attackers and it may indicate an attempt to fool or intercept any data you send to the serve
remediation: Enforce **HTTPS** everywhere with HSTS (max-ageâ‰¥31536000; includeSubDomains; preload), disable weak protocols/ciphers, and use modern TLS configuration with valid certificates.
animated_example: Navigate to the application over HTTPS and inspect the SSL certificate using browser DevTools or testssl.sh
Observe: the certificate is self-signed, expired, or issued for a different domain name
Confirm: browsers display a certificate warning and the certificate cannot be validated against a trusted CA
Assess whether HSTS is configured, noting that an invalid certificate could facilitate MITM interception
Document: invalid SSL certificate — raise as High severity; recommend a valid CA-signed certificate and HSTS

name: Java Servlet Version Disclosure in HTTP Headers
summary: HTTP response headers disclose Java Servlet and JSP version information to unauthenticated clients.
description: The HTTP responses returned by this web application include an header named Java Servlet and JSP version.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported **stable version** and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.
animated_example: Send a standard GET request to the application and inspect the HTTP response headers in Burp Suite
Observe: a response header discloses the Java Servlet and JSP version (e.g., X-Powered-By: JSP/2.x)
Cross-reference the disclosed version against public CVE databases to identify known vulnerabilities
Confirm: the version is exposed to unauthenticated users and aids targeted exploitation
Document: Java Servlet version disclosure — raise as Low/Informational; recommend removing version headers in production config

name: Lack of Robust Input Validation and Output Sanitization
summary: User-supplied input is accepted without strict validation or sanitisation, increasing DOM XSS risk in consuming pages.
description: The user may send malicious characters within parameters which are reflected in the application's response without being sanitized. For this test, a cross-site scripting payload was used. The application is not vulnerable to cross-site scripting due to using the "Content-Type" Application/JSON rather than Text/HTML. Affected URL: https://example.com/saveUser.aspx Impact: Not performing robust input validation and output sanitization increases the risk of DOM-based cross-site scripting attacks where another page uses JavaScript to load content from the affected URL.
remediation: Perform strict input validation on parameters using a whitelist of allowed characters for the expected data type. Perform output sanitization by encoding user input before it is returned in the application's output by using the appropriate encoding. Use HTML encoding for HTML context, URL encoding for URL context, and JavaScript encoding for JavaScript context.
animated_example: Identify parameters reflected in application responses and submit XSS payloads via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the Content-Type is application/json so the browser does not execute scripts, but values are reflected unencoded
Confirm: the unencoded output could be consumed by a downstream page using JavaScript, enabling DOM-based XSS
Review all pages that load data from this endpoint for unsafe JavaScript sinks
Document: lack of input validation — DOM XSS risk in consuming pages; raise as Medium severity; recommend strict encoding

name: LDAP Injection
summary: Application builds LDAP queries using unsanitised user input, enabling LDAP injection attacks.
description: LDAP injection arises when user-controllable data is copied in an unsafe way into an LDAP query that is performed by the application. If an attacker can inject LDAP metacharacters into the query, then they can interfere with the query's logic. Depending on the function for which the query is used, the attacker may be able to retrieve sensitive data to which they are not authorized, or subvert the application's logic to perform some unauthorized action.
remediation: Applications should avoid copying user-controllable data into LDAP queries. If this is unavoidable, then the data should be strictly validated to prevent LDAP injection attacks. In most situations, it will be appropriate to allow only short alphanumeric strings to be copied into queries, and any other input should be rejected. At a minimum, input containing any LDAP metacharacters should be rejected; characters that should be blocked include ( ) ; , * | & = and whitespace.
animated_example: Identify input fields used for authentication or directory lookups that may be passed to an LDAP query
Submit LDAP metacharacter payloads such as *)(uid=*))(|(uid=* in the username or search field via Burp Suite
Observe: the application returns unexpected results — all users, authentication bypass, or an error revealing query structure
Confirm: the application constructs LDAP queries by concatenating user input without escaping metacharacters
Document: LDAP injection confirmed — authentication bypass and data exposure risk; raise as Critical severity

name: Local File Inclusion
summary: Application includes arbitrary local files based on a user-supplied parameter, exposing server-side files.
description: The application is vulnerable to local file inclusion. Affected Parameters (URLs): url (https://example.com/fetchdata.aspx) Impact: A malicious user may view arbitrary files on the web server, such as source code, configuration files, and any sensitive data stored on the server.
remediation: Perform input validation checks to ensure that only the intended files may be viewed. Do not allow the user to view files within other directories.
animated_example: Identify a parameter that references a file path or page name (e.g., ?page=home, ?url=report.pdf)
Submit path traversal payloads: ../../../../etc/passwd or ../../../../windows/win.ini via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the server returns the contents of the referenced system file in the response
Confirm: the application includes local files based on user input without validating to an allowed file list
Document: local file inclusion — arbitrary file read; raise as Critical severity; recommend allowlist-based file inclusion

name: Malicious File Upload
summary: Application accepts malicious file uploads including server-side scripts, enabling remote code execution.
description: It has been identified that the user is able to upload malicious files like php reverse shell and successfully saves in the database
remediation: It is recommended to restrcited the dangerous file extensions by performing validation both at client and server end
animated_example: Navigate to the file upload functionality and attempt to upload a PHP reverse shell or ASPX webshell file
Observe: the server accepts the file without validating the extension, MIME type, or file content
Confirm the uploaded file is stored in a web-accessible directory by navigating to the expected upload path
Confirm: the server processes and executes the uploaded script, demonstrating remote code execution
Document: malicious file upload — RCE risk; raise as Critical severity; recommend strict extension and MIME type validation

name: Mass assignment
summary: API binds all client-supplied fields to the internal object model without filtering, enabling mass assignment.
description: Mass assignment occurs when an API automatically binds client-provided data to internal objects without proper filtering. This can allow attackers to modify sensitive properties they shouldn't have access to, leading to potential security breaches
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Review the API documentation or responses to identify internal model fields not exposed in normal requests
Submit a registration or update request including additional fields (e.g., isAdmin: true, role: 'admin') via Burp Suite
Observe: the server accepts and persists the extra fields, assigning elevated permissions to the created/updated object
Confirm: the API binds all request body properties to the model without a defined allowlist of accepted fields
Document: mass assignment confirmed — privilege escalation risk; raise as High severity; recommend explicit field allowlisting

name: Microsoft IIS Server service.cnf and services.cnf file found
summary: IIS configuration files (service.cnf/services.cnf) are publicly accessible and may disclose sensitive information.
description: Microsoft Internet Information Services (IIS) installs a file named service.cnf. This file may contain sensitive information, which could allow a remote attacker to launch further attacks.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Navigate to common IIS configuration file paths (e.g., /service.cnf, /services.cnf) via Burp Suite or browser
Observe: the files are accessible without authentication and return configuration or credential data
Review the file contents for sensitive information such as credentials, connection strings, or internal paths
Confirm: the configuration files are web-accessible and not protected by access controls
Document: IIS config file exposed — information disclosure; raise as High severity; recommend removing or restricting access

name: Microsoft IIS tilde directory enumeration
summary: Microsoft IIS tilde enumeration allows discovery of short-form filenames and directory names on the server.
description: It is possible to detect short names of files and directories which have an 8.3 file naming scheme equivalent in Windows by using some vectors in several versions of Microsoft IIS.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported **stable version** and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.
animated_example: Use a tilde enumeration tool (e.g., IIS ShortName Scanner) against the target IIS server
Send requests with tilde patterns (e.g., /abc~1.asp) and observe the difference in HTTP response codes
Observe: 404 vs 200/400 response code differences reveal the existence of files and directories with 8.3 short names
Confirm: hidden or sensitive filenames (backup files, configuration files) are enumerable via the tilde technique
Document: IIS tilde enumeration — file discovery risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend disabling 8.3 filename generation

name: MicrosoftSharePointTeamServices
summary: MicrosoftSharePointTeamServices response header discloses the SharePoint version to unauthenticated clients.
description: The value of this header disclose the version of MicrosoftSharePointTeamServices in use. It is not necessary for production sites and should be disabled.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported **stable version** and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.
animated_example: Send a GET request to the application and inspect all HTTP response headers in Burp Suite
Observe: the MicrosoftSharePointTeamServices header is present in the response, disclosing the SharePoint version number
Cross-reference the disclosed version against known SharePoint CVEs and security advisories
Confirm: the version is exposed to unauthenticated users and enables targeted exploitation of known vulnerabilities
Document: SharePoint version header disclosure — raise as Low/Informational; recommend removing the header in production config

name: Missing Anti-Automation Control
summary: Application enforces no rate limiting, enabling automated brute-force or denial-of-service attacks on login.
description: The application does not enforce any rate limiting of user requests, allowing for the possibility of denial of service (DOS) or brute-force password guessing attacks. Affected URL: https://example.com/login Impact: A malicious user may gain access to [user accounts] by submitting a large number of [login attempts].
remediation: Enforce rate limiting as a control against automated tools performing a large number of [authentication requests].
animated_example: Navigate to the login endpoint and capture a login request in Burp Suite Intruder
Configure Intruder to send 100+ requests with a password wordlist for a known valid username with no delays
Observe: all requests are processed without triggering a lockout, CAPTCHA, or HTTP 429 rate limit response
Confirm: no anti-automation control is enforced — unlimited authentication attempts are accepted
Document: missing anti-automation control — raise as High severity; recommend rate limiting and account lockout

name: Missing Anti-Scripting Control
summary: Form lacks CAPTCHA or anti-scripting control, allowing automated mass submission and inbox flooding.
description: The application's FORM NAME form does not use an anti-scripting control, such as a CAPTCHA, to prevent automated submissions. Affected URL: https://example.com/login Impact: A malicious user may use an automated tool to send a large number of requests to the FORM NAME form. The specified email inbox may be flooded with messages.
remediation: Place an anti-scripting control on the form, such as a CAPTCHA. Validate that the CAPTCHA is solved correctly and invalidate the CAPTCHA after it is used.
animated_example: Navigate to the unprotected form (e.g., contact, registration, login) and capture the submission request in Burp Suite
Send the request repeatedly using Burp Repeater or Intruder without providing any CAPTCHA token
Observe: the server processes all submissions without requiring a CAPTCHA solution or applying rate limiting
Confirm: no anti-scripting control is present — the form accepts unlimited automated submissions
Document: missing anti-scripting control — DoS and spam risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend server-validated CAPTCHA

name: Missing Cache Control Header & Pragma Header
summary: Responses serving sensitive data lack Cache-Control and Pragma headers, permitting local caching.
description: Unless directed otherwise, browsers may store a local cached copy of content received from web servers. If sensitive information in application responses is stored in the local cache, then this may be retrieved by other users who have access to the same computer at a future time
remediation:
Prevent caching by adding "Cache Control" and "Pragma:" to the page headers
• Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate, pre-check=0, post-check=0, max-age=0, s-maxage=0
• Pragma: no-cache
animated_example: Navigate to pages containing sensitive data and capture the HTTP responses in Burp Suite
Inspect response headers for Cache-Control: no-store and Pragma: no-cache directives
Observe: sensitive responses are served without caching prevention headers
Confirm: the browser stores a local copy of the sensitive response by checking the browser cache after navigation
Document: missing cache control headers — sensitive data cached locally; raise as Medium severity; recommend no-store/no-cache

name: Missing Character Length Validation
summary: Input fields accept unlimited character lengths, potentially enabling denial-of-service via oversized payloads.
description: An attacker may flood the input field with huge amount of character and there by crashing the server
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Identify input fields and submit progressively larger payloads (1KB, 10KB, 100KB) via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the server accepts extremely long input strings without returning a validation error or truncating input
Monitor server response time and behaviour for signs of resource exhaustion under large input sizes
Confirm: no server-side character length limit is enforced on the targeted input field
Document: missing character length validation — DoS risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend max-length enforcement server-side

name: Missing Character Limit Validation
summary: Application input fields have no character limit enforced, allowing oversized submissions.
description: It has been identified that the character limit has not been set in the input field
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Navigate to input fields and submit strings exceeding typical expected lengths via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the server accepts strings of arbitrary length without returning a validation error
Confirm: no server-side maximum character limit is enforced on the field
Assess whether excessively long input causes abnormal server behaviour or resource impact
Document: missing character limit validation — raise as Low/Medium severity; recommend server-side max-length constraints

name: Missing Content Security Policy Header
summary: Application does not set a Content-Security-Policy header, reducing defence against XSS attacks.
description: This response header allows web site administrators to control resources the user agent is allowed to load for a given page. With a few exceptions, policies mostly involve specifying server origins and script endpoints. This helps guard against cross-site scripting attacks (XSS).
remediation: Enforce **contextual output encoding**, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and use frameworks that auto-escape. Avoid using dangerous sinks or sanitize with vetted libraries (e.g., DOMPurify for client-side).
animated_example: Capture HTTP responses from the application and inspect the response headers in Burp Suite
Observe: no Content-Security-Policy header is present in responses from any application page

name: Missing Content-Security-Policy
summary: Content-Security-Policy header is absent from application responses, leaving XSS defences incomplete.
description: The “Content-Security-Policy” HTTP response header is not set in application responses. Impact: Not implementing a content security policy increases the chance that user data may be exposed.
remediation:
Set the "Content-Security-Policy" header within application responses.

Example:
Content-Security-Policy: default src 'none'; block-all-mixed-content; script-src 'self';

Note: The above is a good starting place for a content security policy, but may need to be modified, such as to allow inline scripts, if the application architecture requires.
animated_example: Send requests to key application pages and inspect all HTTP response headers in Burp Suite
Observe: the Content-Security-Policy header is not present in any of the application responses reviewed
Confirm: the absence of CSP means inline scripts, eval, and external resources are unrestricted by the browser
Review whether a meta http-equiv CSP tag exists as an alternative in the page HTML source
Document: missing CSP header — raise as Medium severity; recommend deploying a Content-Security-Policy with strict directives

name: Missing Filename Validation

description: Affected URL: https://example.com/ Impact: It has been identified that the user specified filename is not validated and accepts any random character with no limit specified.
remediation: It is recommended to validate the filename and character limit specified by the user.

Submit filenames containing special characters, path separators, and very long strings via Burp Suite Repeater
Observe: the server accepts filenames with arbitrary characters including directory traversal sequences
Confirm: no server-side filename validation or length restriction is applied
Document: missing filename validation — path traversal and DoS risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend strict filename allowlist

name: Missing HTTP Strict-Transport-Security
summary: Application does not set HSTS header, leaving users vulnerable to protocol downgrade attacks.
description: The “Strict-Transport-Security” HTTP response header is not set in application responses. Impact: The HTTP Strict Transport Security policy defines a timeframe where a browser must connect to the web server via HTTPS. Without a Strict Transport Security policy, the web application may be vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks.
remediation:
A Strict-Transport-Security HTTP header should be sent within each response. The syntax is as follows:

Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains
 
Optionally, include the preload attribute so that browsers will never connect to the site over HTTP, even on the first request. Note, this may render the site unreachable by users if the site switches from HTTPS to HTTP. You may submit your site to the HSTS preload list here at https://hstspreload.org.

Example:
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload
animated_example: Send HTTPS requests to the application and inspect the response headers in Burp Suite
Observe: the Strict-Transport-Security header is absent from application responses
Confirm: without HSTS, a browser will not enforce HTTPS on future visits, enabling HTTPS downgrade attacks
Verify whether the application redirects HTTP to HTTPS and whether the redirect itself sets HSTS
Document: missing HSTS header — protocol downgrade risk; raise as Medium severity; recommend max-age=31536000 with includeSubDomains

name: Missing Secure cookie attributes
summary: Session cookies lack Secure and HttpOnly attributes, exposing them to interception and JavaScript access.
description: secure and http-only attributes need to be added to cookie flags of all pages
remediation: Set **Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite**; avoid sensitive data in cookies; encrypt/sign values and minimize lifetime.
animated_example: Log in to the application and capture the Set-Cookie header in Burp Suite
Inspect the cookie attributes for the presence of Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite flags
Observe: one or more session cookies are set without the Secure and HttpOnly attributes
Confirm: cookies without Secure may be sent over HTTP; cookies without HttpOnly are accessible via JavaScript (XSS)
Document: missing secure cookie attributes — raise as High severity; recommend Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite=Strict

name: Missing Step-Up Authentication
summary: Sensitive account changes such as email updates require no step-up authentication, enabling session-hijack account takeover.
description: The application allows users to update the email address associated with the account without performing a form of step-up authentication, such as verifying the user's current password. Affected URL: https://example.com/update Profile.aspx Impact: A malicious user who gains access to a user's session may update the Email Address and subsequently take over the account.
remediation: Perform step-up authentication for all sensitive functions, such as modifying email addresses or security questions. This may be performed by verifying the user's current password or requesting an OTP.
animated_example: Log in and navigate to the email address update function, capturing the update request in Burp Suite
Submit the email change request without providing the current password or any additional verification
Observe: the server processes the email change without prompting for step-up authentication
Confirm: any session with a valid token can change the account email without knowing the user's password
Document: missing step-up authentication — account takeover risk; raise as High severity; recommend password re-verification

name: Missing Strict-Transport-Security header
summary: Application lacks HSTS header, allowing browsers to connect over HTTP and enabling MITM attacks.
description: HTTP strict transport security [HSTS] enforces the connections in HTTPS disregarding any script's call to load any resource in that domain over HTTP.
remediation: Enforce **HTTPS** everywhere with HSTS (max-ageâ‰¥31536000; includeSubDomains; preload), disable weak protocols/ciphers, and use modern TLS configuration with valid certificates.
animated_example: Capture HTTPS responses from the application and inspect the response headers in Burp Suite
Observe: the Strict-Transport-Security header is absent from all application responses
Attempt to connect to the application over plain HTTP and confirm it does not enforce a redirect in all browsers
Confirm: without HSTS, browsers may make the initial request over HTTP, exposing session cookies to interception
Document: missing HSTS header — MITM risk on first connection; raise as Medium severity; recommend HSTS with preload

name: Missing X-Content-Type-Options
summary: X-Content-Type-Options header is missing, allowing browsers to MIME-sniff responses and execute content unexpectedly.
description: Setting this header Will prevent the browser from MIME-sniffing a response away from the declared content-type.
remediation: Apply **secure defaults**: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
animated_example: Capture HTTP responses from the application and inspect response headers in Burp Suite
Observe: the X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header is absent from application responses

Document: missing X-Content-Type-Options — raise as Low severity; recommend adding X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff

name: Missing X-Frame-Options Header
summary: Application lacks X-Frame-Options header, enabling clickjacking via iframe embedding on third-party pages.
description: The web server did not return an X-Frame-Options header which means that this website could be at risk of a clickjacking attack.
remediation: Send **X-Frame-Options: DENY** (or SAMEORIGIN as needed) and a restrictive **Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors** directive.
animated_example: Capture a response from the application and inspect the response headers in Burp Suite
Observe: the X-Frame-Options header is absent and no frame-ancestors directive is set in Content-Security-Policy
Create a test HTML page that embeds the application URL in an iframe to confirm it renders without restriction
Confirm: the application can be framed by arbitrary third-party origins, enabling UI redress attacks
Document: missing X-Frame-Options — raise as Medium severity; recommend X-Frame-Options: DENY or CSP frame-ancestors

name: Missing XSS Protection Header
summary: X-XSS-Protection header is absent, depriving older browsers of their built-in XSS filter as a defence layer.
description: X-XSS-protection header helps in enabling the cross-site scripting (XSS) filter built on browsers.
remediation: Enforce **contextual output encoding**, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and use frameworks that auto-escape. Avoid using dangerous sinks or sanitize with vetted libraries (e.g., DOMPurify for client-side).
animated_example: Capture HTTP responses from the application and inspect the response headers in Burp Suite
Observe: the X-XSS-Protection header is not present in application responses
Confirm: older browser XSS filter protection is not activated by the application as an additional layer
Note: modern browsers have deprecated this header; primary XSS mitigation should rely on CSP and output encoding
Document: missing X-XSS-Protection — raise as Low/Informational; recommend X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block as supplemental

name: No Character Set Specified
summary: Application responses do not declare a character set, increasing XSS risk through charset sniffing.
description: Not specifying a character set increases the risk of client-side attacks, such as cross-site scripting.
remediation: Enforce **contextual output encoding**, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and use frameworks that auto-escape. Avoid using dangerous sinks or sanitize with vetted libraries (e.g., DOMPurify for client-side).

Test whether encoding-based XSS payloads (e.g., UTF-7) execute in the context of the missing charset declaration
Document: no character set specified — raise as Low severity; recommend charset=UTF-8 in all Content-Type headers
